coredata.dc:title: Layer-by-layer assembled PEI-based vector with the upconversion luminescence marker for gene delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yaqiong
Cao, Ping
Li, Shicui
Zhang, Xiaofeng
Hu, Jin
Yang, Mingyue
Yao, Sujuan
Gao, Feng
Xia, Ao
Shen, Jian
Huang, Xiaohua
coredata.dc:description: The upconversion luminescence (UCL) marker based on upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) shows unique advantages over traditional fluorescence markers, such as enhanced tissue penetration, better photostability, and less autofluorescence. Herein, we constructed a new UCL gene-delivery nonviral vector via layer-by-layer self-assembly of poly(ethylene imine) (PEI) with UCNPs. To reduce the cytotoxicity of PEI, citric acid (CA) was introduced for aqueous modification, and PEI assembly was introduced on the UCNP surface. Our data show that the nonviral vector for UCL gene-delivery demonstrates excellent photostability, low toxicity, and good stability under physiological or serum conditions and can strongly bind to DNA. Moreover, this UCL PEI-based vector could serve as a promising fluorescent gene-delivery carrier for theranostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gene delivery
Gene vector
Layer-by-layer assembly
Upconversion nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI gene-delivery vector
Evaluation of the binding ability of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI to pDNA
Stability of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites
In vitro transfection and UCL imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI; (B) UCL emission spectra of (a) UCNPs dissolved in cyclohexane and (b) UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI dispersed in water excited with a CW 980 nm laser. Insert photo exhibited the luminescence photographs of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI dissolved in water with the CW 980 nm excitation.
In vitro cell viability of HeLa incubated with different concentrations of samples for 24 h.

(A) Agarose Gel Electrophoresis of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA. (1) Without UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI NPs as the control group; (2-6) UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA with N/P = 2, 5, 10, 20, and 30, respectively; (B) results of transfection efficiency of pure pDNA, PEI, UCNP@CA@PEI and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@ in HeLa cells for 24 h.

The UCL imaging of HeLa cells incubated with UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA. (A) Bright-field image; (B) fluorescence image; (C) overlay-field image; (D) 3D surface plot of the UCL intensity distribution for the top right corner of the UCL image of HeLa cells; (E) the luminescence gray value distribution from a → b.

head.ce:title: Layer-by-layer assembled PEI-based vector with the upconversion luminescence marker for gene delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials, Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials, School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Wenyuan Road 1, Nanjing, 210046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center of Biomedical Functional Materials
Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Biomedical Materials
School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Jahwa United Co., Ltd, Baoding Road 527, Shanghai, 200082, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jinling Hospital, Zhongshandonglu Road 305, Nanjing, 210002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The upconversion luminescence (UCL) marker based on upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) shows unique advantages over traditional fluorescence markers, such as enhanced tissue penetration, better photostability, and less autofluorescence. Herein, we constructed a new UCL gene-delivery nonviral vector via layer-by-layer self-assembly of poly(ethylene imine) (PEI) with UCNPs. To reduce the cytotoxicity of PEI, citric acid (CA) was introduced for aqueous modification, and PEI assembly was introduced on the UCNP surface. Our data show that the nonviral vector for UCL gene-delivery demonstrates excellent photostability, low toxicity, and good stability under physiological or serum conditions and can strongly bind to DNA. Moreover, this UCL PEI-based vector could serve as a promising fluorescent gene-delivery carrier for theranostic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Gene delivery is the key to gene therapy because of the need for repeated therapeutic administration [1–4]. Significant research on gene vectors has been performed to understand the genetic basis of diseases and advances for gene therapeutic purposes. Compared with the high efficiency of viral vectors for gene delivery, nonviral vectors have their advantages due to simple use, ease of production, less concerns, and the lack of specific immune response [5–7]. Poly(ethylene imine) (PEI)-based nonviral vectors are of high efficiency in gene delivery, and varied architecture PEI vectors such as hydrogels, microspheres, nanospheres and scaffolds, which can encapsulate and deliver functional genes, have been developed and used for gene delivery [8–10]. However, the toxicity of PEI-based vectors is a serious challenge to safe and successful gene therapy.

Fluorescent gene-delivery vectors provide a visual tool to obverse and trace gene delivery into cells [11,12]. Many nanoparticle-based gene-delivery vectors, such as fluorescent quantum dots, carbon dots and gold nanoparticles, not only can be engineered for effective gene delivery but also allow real-time tracking or imaging-guided gene therapy [13–15]. Lanthanide-doped, rare-earth upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) can be used as a special tracer for gene delivery research because UCNPs can emit visual photons under excitation by near-infrared (NIR) light [16,17]. Compared with traditional fluorescence markers, unique advantages on UCNPs, such as better photostability and less background autofluorescence allow ultrasensitive in vivo or in vitro detection.

Herein, we prepared UCNP-based gene-delivery vector by layer-by-layer self-assembly of citric acid (CA) and two-layer PEI and used the as-prepared UCL gene vector, UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI, complex as an effective gene-delivery vector with UCL marker to transfect plasmid DNA (pDNA). CA not only makes multi-layer modified UCNPs disperse in water very well but also reduces the cytotoxicity of PEI obviously. Agarose gel electrophoresis confirmed that UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI can strongly bind to pDNA. UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites are of good stability under physiological or serum conditions.

Ln2O3 (>99.9%, Ln: Y, Er, Yb), sodium oleate (C18H33NaO2, 98.0%), ammonium fluoride (NH4F, ≧99.99%), polyethylenimine (branched PEI polymer, M.W. 10,000, 99%) and thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98%) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., China. Oleic acid (OA), octadecene (ODE) and citric acid (Na3C6H5O7·2H2O, 99.0%) were purchased from Sigma Reagent Co., China. Agarose and tris Base (99.8%) were obtained from Bio Sharp Co., China. LnCl3 (Ln: Y, Yb, Er) were prepared by dissolving the corresponding metal oxide in hydrochloric solution (30%), then the water were completely evaporated to acquire powders of LnCl3 (Ln: Y, Yb, Er). Other chemical reagents were of analytical grade, and all chemical reagents were directly used without further purification. The plasmid DNA was purchased from Luli Bio-agent (Nanjing) Co., Ltd.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were measured with a Bruker D4 X-ray diffractometer (Cu Ka radiation, λ = 0.15406 nm). The morphologies of samples were observed on a JEOL JEM-2010F transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) at 200 kV. Samples were prepared by placing a drop of dilute dispersions in cyclohexane and water on the surface of a copper grid respectively. UCL spectra were measured on an Edinburgh LFS-920 spectrometer, where an external 0–1 W adjustable CW laser at 980 nm (Connet Fiber Optics, China) replaced the Xenon lamp as the excitation source. FT-IR spectra were obtained with Shimadzu IR Prestige-21 spectrometer in KBr pellets. Zeta potential of nanoparticles was measured with the Zetasizer Nano (Malvern).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI gene-delivery vector
ce:section.ce:para: NaYF4:Yb3+, Er3+ UCNPs were synthesized followed by our previous reported literatures [18,19] and the preparation details of UCNP@CA, UCNP@CA@PEI, UCNP@2 × CA@PEI and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI were in the supporting information.

The as-prepared UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI was ultrasonically dispersed in a pH 7.4 PBS solution with serum. Based on the N/P = 2, 5, 10, 20, and 30, the different concentrations of nanoparticles were slowly added into an equal volume of pDNA solution. Then the mixtures were shaken 30min, after allowed to stand for 30 min at 25 °C. Finally, obtaining UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites. The N/P value is the ratio of the number of moles of nitrogen in the PEI to the number of moles of phosphorus in the pDNA [20,21]:
N/P= PEI (μg) /pDNA (μg) × 7.75

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the binding ability of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI to pDNA
ce:section.ce:para: Evaluation of the binding ability to pDNA by the measurement of Zeta potential of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI in the pH 6.0, 7.0 and 7.4 PBS with serum and the agarose gel electrophoresis experiment. For the agarose gel electrophoresis, separately taking 10 μL UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites with different N/P values, and dropping into 10 × TAE buffer. Then performing electrophoresis at 100 V for 50 min in a 1% agarose gel. Meanwhile, an equal volume of pDNA solution was added to the same volume of 10 × TAE buffer solution as a control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Selecting N/P = 20 UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA as test samples. The samples were diluted and dispersed in DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and placed at different time. Then the stability of the UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites was detected by using dynamic light scattering (DLS).

HeLa cells were cultivated into 96-well culture pates for 24 h at 37 °C under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. Next, Different concentrations of UCNP@CA@PEI, UCNP@2 × CA@PEI and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA (2、4、8、16、31、6、125 μg/mL) were added to the 96-well experimental group cells and incubated for 24 h. 5 mg/ml MTT solution was added to each well and the plate was incubated for 4 h under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. Subsequently, after the addition 200 μL DMSO in each well, the assay plate was allowed to stand 2 h at room temperature. The optical density OD 570 value of each well was measured by a multifunctional microplate reader after subtracting 490 nm wavelength background. The following formula was used to calculate the viability of cell growth:
Cell viability (%) = (mean of Abs. value of treatment group/mean Abs. value of control) × 100%

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro transfection and UCL imaging
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were seeded in 24-well plates at a density of 1 × 106 cells per well in 1 mL of complete DMEM medium supplement with 10% FBS (fetal bovine serum) at 37 °C under 5% CO2, and 95% relative humidity overnight before transfection. The culture medium was washed with serum free DMEM medium, and transfection PEI@pDNA, UCNP@CA@PEI@pDNA and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA were added to the cells. The cells were incubated with the transfection complexes at 37 °C for an additional 24 h after the medium was replaced by fresh complete medium. After incubation for 24 h, the medium was aspirated and washed with phosphate-buffered saline. The cells were trypsinized and then the transfection results were measured by fluorescence activated cell sorting on a FACS Calibur flow cytometer (BD Biosciences).

HeLa Cells (5 × 108/L) were plated on 14 mm glass coverslips and allowed to adhere for 24 h. Subsequently, after being washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS), the cells were incubated in PBS buffer containing 1.0 mL UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA (30 mg/mL) at 37 °C for 1 h under 5% CO2. And then cells were sufficiently washed with PBS to remove excess nanocomposites.

UCL imaging of living cells were performed on our laser scanning upconversion luminescence microscope (LSUCLM) under the excitation of CW 980 nm laser [22]. The bright-field images were obtained under the light of 534 nm, UCL signals were collected in the channel of 450 nm–500 nm.

In this study, NaYF4-based UCNPs (Y:Yb:Er = 78:20:2) were prepared as a fluorescent tracer of gene-delivery vectors. The as-prepared UCNPs are hydrophobic and can be well dispersed in non-polar organic solutions. TEM images of UCNPs show that the morphology of UCNPs is spherical with an average diameter of ∼10 nm, and the nanoparticles are highly and uniformly monodispersible (Fig. S1A). We further characterized the size of the nanoparticles by dynamic light scattering (DLS). As shown in Fig. S2A, the mean size of UCNPs dispersed in cyclohexane is approximately 13 nm. The size of the nanoparticles as measured by DLS is slightly larger than that measured by TEM due to the solvent effect. HRTEM (Fig. S1B) reveals that the UCNP interplanar spacing is 0.512 nm, consistent with that of β-NaYF4 (100). Additionally, Fourier transform diffraction of the UCNPs is illustrated. Clear electron diffraction spots can be seen (Insert, Fig. S1B), further indicating that UCNPs are of good crystallinity. The crystallinity of the nanoparticles also was explored by XRD (Fig. S3). Yb3+, Er3+ co-doped NaYF4 main peak positions and intensities correspond with standard β-NaYF4 (JCPDS16-0334).

Hydrophilic UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI NPs were prepared by ligand exchange and a layer-by-layer assembly procedure (Scheme S1). As shown in Fig. S1C and Fig. 1 A, UCNP@CA and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI demonstrate that the morphology of nanoparticles do not change, the size is uniform, and the dispersion is good after multi-layer modification. The Zeta potentials of the nanoparticles (Fig. S4A) are −42.1 mV, +41.4 mV, −36.0 mV and +40.4 mV after the UCNPs are modified by CA and PEI. Unmodified and multilayer modified nanoparticles were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectrometry. From the FTIR spectra (Fig. S4B), we can know the identified peak bands at approximately 3352 cm−1, 1560 cm−1, and 1130 cm−1 that can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of –NH, -CO, and -CN, respectively. The peak at 1658 cm−1 correspond to –NH bending vibration. In addition, the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) spectra of UCNPs and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI (Fig. S4C) show the weight loss of OA and CA, and the PEI values on the surface of samples are measured to approximately 5.86% and 8.93%, respectively. These findings reveal that CA and PEI had replaced oleic acid ligands and are assembled on the surface of nanoparticles.

Fig. 1B shows the UCL spectrum of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI in water, and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI can emit yellow green UCL light under continuous wave (CW) excitation at 980 nm. From the experimental results, UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI is of good luminescent properties and can be applied for gene delivery as fluorescent marker.

The stability of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI was evaluated through measuring the Zeta potential of nanoparticles dispersed in different pH PBS solutions [23]. Fig. S4D shows that the UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI has a significant positive charge in pH 6.0, 7.0 and 7.4 PBS solutions, presenting that UCNP@2 × PEI NPs have high stability. In addition, we carried out acid-base titration experiments to study its buffering capacity [24]. Fig. S5 shows that the amount of 0.1 M HCl solution required for UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI is between water and PEI, but closer to PEI (w: 10,000). The above results indicate that UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI have a buffering capacity and can release pDNA from endosomes or lysosomes.

The hydrodynamic diameter of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites (Fig. S6) was measured by DLS to confirm their stability in DMEM medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum, after being placed at room temperature for 0 h, 0.5 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 24 h and 48 h. The results show that the hydrodynamic diameter for nanocomposites increases for the first 4 h, and there are no significant differences after 4 h until 2 days. This implies that the UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites have stability that may be suitable in a long-term storage.

To evaluate the cytotoxicity of as-prepared PEI-based nanocomposites, HeLa cell viability was measured using the MTT assay [25]. As shown in Fig. 2 , the viability of the HeLa cells is greater than 80% after incubating with 0–125 μg/mL of nanocomposites for 24 h, when CA is introduced twice on the PEI-based nanocomposites. However, the viability of the HeLa cells is less than 60% after incubating with 0–125 μg/mL of UCNP@CA@PEI for 24 h. These findings suggest that CA can reduce the cytotoxicity of PEI-based vector, and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI NPs can be used as a gene-delivery vector for gene therapy research.

As shown in Fig. 3 A, agarose gel electrophoresis of the UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites indicates that, when the N/P value is more than 20, there is no free pDNA band in the lane, indicating that the UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI nanoparticles are almost completely bound to pDNA. The results suggest that UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI has a strong binding ability to pDNA and can be used as a gene carrier [26].

The ability of the UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI nanocomposites to transfect HeLa cells using pDNA containing the luciferase and protein (GFP) gene was investigated and compared to pure pDNA and PEI@pDNA complexes. The uptake of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI nanocomposites for HeLa cell is 60% at 6 h, 82% at 24 h, and 85% at 36 h. As shown in Fig. 3B, the pure pDNA is barely transfected (0.75 ± 0.32 × 104 relative light units (RLU) mg−1) in HeLa cells, whereas both UCNP@CA@PEI (4.69 ± 2.12 × 105 RLU mg−1) and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI (6.02 ± 1.97 × 105 RLU mg−1) can achieve a higher degree of transfection, which is slight less than the PEI (6.25 ± 1.63 × 105 RLU mg−1).

The application to trace pDNA delivery to living cells in vitro was demonstrated by HeLa cells on a LSUCLM, after UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA was endocytosed intracellularly. As shown in Fig. 4 , UCL imaging of living HeLa cells was studied by incubating them with 100 μg/mL of UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites, followed by laser scanning upconversion luminescence microscopy (LSUCLM). Bright-field image (Fig. 4A) and FM image (Fig. 4B) are completely covered, indicating that the UCL signal comes from the intracellular region of HeLa cells (Fig. 4C). Furthermore, a quantitative analysis of the intensity of the UCL signal and the background was made to present the UCL SNR after UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA was endocytosed in the living cells. Fig. 4D and E show the UCL intensity distribution for the imaging of HeLa cells, corresponding to the area marked with the red line in the fluorescence image of the HeLa cells. Three points are selected for analysis of the SNR of the UCL images. Normalized counts of the UCL signal of point c from cell is about 90, whereas the counts of the background noise at points a and b are close to 0. The calculated SNR for living cell imaging is more than 300. Such high SNR strongly suggests that UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI is of low background fluorescent as a fluorescent gene-delivery vector.

In summary, the high-performance PEI-based gene vector with the UCL marker was prepared by ligand exchange and layer-by-layer self-assembly on the UCNPs. This PEI-based UCL gene-delivery vector is of high stability and good buffer capacity and can be effectively combined with pDNA for gene therapy research. In addition, the cytotoxicity of PEI-based UCL gene-delivery vector and UCNP@2 × CA@2 × PEI@pDNA nanocomposites is low due to the layer of CA. In vitro imaging proves that there is very low autofluorescence, when PEI-based UCL vector is used to monitor the delivery of pDNA. The results indicate that the PEI-based vector constructed with UCNPs can be used for application of gene delivery in gene therapy potentially.

We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work. There is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in the manuscript entitled “Layer-by-layer Assembled PEI-based Vector with the Upconversion Luminescence Marker for Gene Delivery”.
All the authors in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support of the National Science Foundation of China (21574067) and the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BK 20151543). The authors are grateful to Professor Fuyou Li (Fudan University) for his valuable instruction and help.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bbrc.2018.07.007.
